name: Castillo de Feria and Salvatierra de los Barros Loop from Zafra
name_original: Salvatierra de los Barros y Castillo de Feria desde Zafra — circular por la Tierra de Barros
description: A difficult 54.1-mile road cycling route from Zafra through Tierra de Barros, featuring the historic Castillo de Feria and scenic plains.
region_intro: Road cycling around Zafra offers diverse landscapes across the province of Badajoz, Spain. The region features varied terrain, from the challenging ascents of the Sierra Grande de Hornachos and Tentudía Mountain Range to the rolling plains characterized by holm and cork oaks. Cyclists can expect continuous ascents and descents, providing a mix of physical challenges and scenic backdrops. This topography supports a range of road cycling experiences, from demanding mountain passes to more gentle routes through agricultural areas. Best road cycling routes around Zafra The most popular road cycling route is Castillo de Feria and Salvatierra de los Barros Loop from Zafra, a 54.2 miles (87.1 km) trail that takes 4 hours 38 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Zafra Old Town – El Capricho de Cotrina loop from Zafra Feria, a moderate 36.8 miles (59.2 km) path. This route offers a mix of rolling hills and open landscapes around Zafra. Local road cyclists also love the Puebla de Sancho Pérez – Zafra Old Town loop from Zafra, a 24.9 miles (40.0 km) trail leading through the plains and agricultural areas, often completed in about 1 hour 50 minutes. Road cycling around Zafra is defined by the Sierra Grande de Hornachos, the Tentudía Mountain Range, and the extensive plains dotted with holm and cork oaks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to difficult climbs. The routes in Zafra are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 389 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Zafra's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Zafra?
A: There are over 45 road cycling routes available around Zafra, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences. These routes vary greatly in length, elevation gain, and difficulty.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes near Zafra?
A: Road cycling around Zafra offers diverse landscapes. You can expect a mix of challenging ascents in areas like the Sierra Grande de Hornachos and the Tentudía Mountain Range, as well as rolling plains characterized by holm and cork oaks. The region is known for its 'continuo subir y bajar' (continuous up and down), providing varied physical challenges and scenic backdrops.

Q: What are the difficulty levels of road cycling routes in Zafra?
A: The road cycling routes around Zafra cater to various skill levels. There are 5 easy routes, 22 moderate routes, and 18 difficult routes. This variety ensures that both beginners and experienced cyclists can find suitable challenges.

Q: Are there any significant natural features or mountain ranges to explore by road bike near Zafra?
A: Yes, Zafra is strategically located near significant natural features. The &lt;a href="https://visit-western-spain.com/sierra-grande-de-hornachos-mountain-range/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sierra Grande de Hornachos&lt;/a&gt; offers picturesque views of vast plains and rocky projections. To the south, the Tentudía Mountain Range provides challenging rides with significant elevation gains through forests of oak, pine, and chestnut.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I see while road cycling around Zafra?
A: Many routes around Zafra offer a blend of natural beauty and historical significance. You can cycle along sections of the historic Vía de la Plata, passing through charming medieval towns. Within Zafra itself, you might encounter landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5334005" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parador Nacional Duques de Feria&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3804712" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Francisco Tower of Zafra&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6458193" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Plaza Grande&lt;/a&gt;. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14621578" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zafra Old Town – El Capricho de Cotrina loop from Zafra Feria&lt;/a&gt;, for example, takes you past the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4270417" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Capricho de Cotrina&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling routes suitable for all ages around Zafra?
A: Yes, Zafra offers several family-friendly road cycling routes, particularly among the 5 easy routes available. These routes often traverse the flatter plains and agricultural areas, providing a more relaxed experience. A good example of a moderate route that can be enjoyed by families looking for a slightly longer ride is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14622885" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puebla de Sancho Pérez – Zafra Old Town loop from Zafra&lt;/a&gt;, which leads through plains and agricultural areas.

Q: Can I find loop road cycling routes around Zafra?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Zafra are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end your ride in the same location. Popular loop routes include &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991635407/castillo-de-feria-and-salvatierra-de-los-barros-loop-from-zafra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castillo de Feria and Salvatierra de los Barros Loop from Zafra&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14621578" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Zafra Old Town – El Capricho de Cotrina loop from Zafra Feria&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy the most about road cycling in Zafra?
A: The road cycling routes in Zafra are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from challenging climbs to scenic routes through holm and cork oak landscapes, and the opportunity to combine physical activity with cultural exploration of historic towns.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Zafra?
A: While specific weather data isn't provided, the region's climate suggests that spring and autumn are generally the most pleasant seasons for road cycling in Zafra. Temperatures are milder, and the natural landscapes are vibrant. Summers can be very hot, making early morning or late afternoon rides preferable, while winters are typically mild but can have cooler, wetter days.

Q: Are there options for parking when road cycling around Zafra?
A: Zafra, as a town, offers various parking options. For specific routes, it's advisable to check the starting points for available parking facilities. Many routes, especially those starting from Zafra itself, will have access to town parking.

Q: Can I bring my bike on public transport in the Zafra region?
A: Policies for carrying bikes on public transport (buses, trains) can vary by operator and region in Spain. It's recommended to check directly with local public transport providers in Extremadura for their specific regulations regarding bicycles, especially for longer journeys or during peak times.

Q: Are there any bike-friendly cafes or accommodation options in Zafra?
A: Zafra is a town with a strong historical and tourist presence, so you can expect to find various cafes and accommodation options. While specific 'bike-friendly' certifications aren't detailed, many establishments are generally welcoming to outdoor enthusiasts. It's always a good idea to check reviews or call ahead to confirm amenities like secure bike storage if needed.
tips: Views of the town of Feria from the approach to the town on the road that comes from Fuente del Maestre.
Perched on top of a hill overlooking the city, Feria's medieval castle is famous for its massive keep. A defensive castle dominates the landscape below.

Salvatierra de los Barros is a municipality known internationally for what has been one of its economic pillars for centuries: pottery. Along with the ceramic industry, the breeding of the Iberian pig raised in freedom among holm oaks and cork oaks stands out.

faqs.question: What is the estimated cycling time for this route?
faqs.answer: This challenging road cycling route is estimated to take approximately 4 hours and 38 minutes of active riding time. However, given its 'difficult' rating and significant elevation gain, you should plan for a longer overall trip, including breaks.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the start of the route in Zafra?
faqs.answer: The route begins and ends in Zafra. While specific parking details aren't provided, Zafra is a town with various parking options. It's advisable to look for public parking areas or street parking near the town center to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as 'difficult' for race biking and requires a high level of physical fitness. It involves significant elevation gain (over 1300 meters) and can include sections with challenging terrain, potentially requiring you to push your bike. It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily designed for road cycling, traversing the rolling hills and wide pastures of the Tierra de Barros region. While mostly paved, some descriptions of paths in the vicinity of Feria mention 'very broken path with many loose stones,' suggesting that certain sections or connecting paths could be unpaved or in poor condition. Be prepared for varied surfaces.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable sights. Key highlights include panoramic Views of Feria, the historic Castillo de Feria, and the charming settlement of Salvatierra de los Barros. The route also offers views of the Sierra Vieja, the Villalba reservoir, and the extensive plains of Tierra de Barros.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The Extremadura region, where this route is located, can experience very hot summers. For a more comfortable and enjoyable ride, spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) are generally the best seasons, offering milder temperatures and pleasant conditions for cycling.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route briefly intersects with several other official trails. You'll find sections that overlap with the [CIMA BD01] Castillo de Feria, the Camino de Santiago del Sur, and the Mtb Vía de la Plata.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It utilizes public roads and paths through the region.

faqs.question: Can I bring my dog on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is designed for race biking. While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on public roads, bringing a dog on a challenging 87 km racebike route is generally not practical or safe for the animal. If you wish to bring a dog, consider shorter, less demanding routes more suitable for walking or off-road cycling with a pet.

faqs.question: What gear should I pack for this challenging ride?
faqs.answer: Given the route's 'difficult' rating and length, it's crucial to be well-prepared. Pack plenty of water and snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling attire for varying weather, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. Due to potentially broken sections, robust tires are advisable.

faqs.question: Are there places to refuel or find refreshments along the way?
faqs.answer: The route passes through settlements like Salvatierra de los Barros and near Feria. These towns are likely to offer opportunities for refreshments, such as cafes or small shops, where you can refuel. However, it's always wise to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer, more challenging sections.
